name: tennessee-eastman

data_origin.blend: TE-process simulator with PI controllers + specific kinetic model

env_axis.name: mode-and-fault
env_axis.shift_mechanism: cross-mode (regime change) and cross-fault (perturbation injection) — both are deviations from baseline operating point

ood_protocol.name: te-cross-mode
ood_protocol.ref: Reinartz et al. 2021 (extended TE); Downs & Vogel 1993 (original TE)

facts: Chemical-process simulator with 28 fault types × 6 operating modes (Reinartz 2021 ext.); 11 manipulated + 41 measured variables.
hypotheses: The (mode × fault) combinations are not all behaviorally distinct; degeneracies collapse the effective cardinality below 6×28.
The simulator's PI-controller architecture leaks into the OOD signal as a hidden confounder common to all environments.
intuitions: Tennessee-Eastman likely appears in the 2023-25 causal-discovery literature.
Ablations that replace specific simulator components (kinetics, controller) to expose simulator-dependent results probably exist.
unknowns: 'Mode' vs 'fault' are both deviations from baseline; the dataset's hierarchy (mode = expected operating regime; fault = unexpected perturbation) is a curated taxonomy, and there is no established label distinguishing 'expected' from 'unexpected' perturbation as separate shift types.
epistemic_state.boundary: A simulator dataset with Ty=3 whose heterogeneity comes entirely from the simulator's own variable naming — typed structure is generated, not discovered.

representational_commitments: Mode-vs-fault taxonomy is the curator's distinction
